description: Evaluates whether the CEO agent correctly routes tasks to the appropriate lead, maintains pipeline ordering, provides sufficient context in dispatch briefs, synthesizes lead results coherently, and respects the hierarchical lane boundaries without absorbing lead work or bypassing the chain of command.

specialist_routing.description: CEO routes tasks to the correct lead based on the nature of the work. Strategic scoping goes to scoper_lead, architecture design goes to architect_lead, implementation goes to builder_lead, and verification goes to verifier_lead. When a request spans multiple lanes, the CEO decomposes it into per-lane dispatches to the appropriate leads.
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: A request to 'decide what to build next' dispatches to scoper_lead. A request to 'design the architecture for this approved slice' dispatches to architect_lead. A request to 'implement this approved design' dispatches to builder_lead. A request to 'verify this build output' dispatches to verifier_lead. Cross-lane requests are decomposed into separate dispatches to the correct leads.
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool calls where the subagent field matches the correct lead for the work type. Scoping work never dispatches to builder_lead. Build work never dispatches to scoper_lead. The CEO's routing plan (Phase 2) correctly maps request components to lead archetypes before any dispatch occurs.

direct_handling.description: CEO handles simple user-facing tasks directly without unnecessary delegation. Status inquiries, conversational exchanges, clarification responses, and questions that can be answered from the CEO's own context do not trigger lead dispatches. The CEO does not over-delegate trivial interactions.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: A question like 'what did you just do?' or 'summarize the last pipeline run' is answered directly from the CEO's conversation context without dispatching any lead. Clarification responses from the user are processed by the CEO and used to inform the next dispatch, not delegated to a lead for interpretation.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: Agent logs show no task tool calls for simple conversational exchanges, status questions, or clarification processing. The CEO responds directly to the user for these cases. Task tool calls appear only when specialized lead work is required.

lane_boundary_respect.description: CEO dispatches only to lead-level agents (scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, verifier_lead) and never directly to workers (backend_developer_worker, frontend_developer_worker, etc.). The CEO respects the hierarchical structure: CEO dispatches leads, leads dispatch workers. The CEO does not skip the lead layer.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: Every task tool call targets one of the four lead archetypes. No task tool call targets a worker-level agent. The CEO never attempts to dispatch researcher_worker, business_analyst_worker, solution_architect_worker, backend_developer_worker, frontend_developer_worker, test_engineer_worker, quantitative_developer_worker, or agentic_engineer_worker directly.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool subagent fields containing only scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, or verifier_lead. Zero task tool calls target any worker-level agent name. The CEO's permission model shows allow only for the four leads and ask for all others.

context_provision.description: CEO provides sufficient context in dispatch briefs for the receiving lead to execute without needing to ask clarifying questions. This includes upstream artifacts, user intent, scope boundaries, constraints, and relevant system state. The brief conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema with all required fields populated.
context_provision.expected_outcome: Dispatch briefs contain all 10 required schema fields: Objective, Lead role being engaged, Upstream artifacts, User intent, Scope boundary, Constraints, Output expected, Pipeline position, Stop condition, and Execution discipline. Per-lead additional required fields are also present (e.g., architect dispatch includes Approved Strategic Slice Brief and Quality attribute priorities).
context_provision.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool query payloads that contain structured dispatch briefs with all mandatory fields populated. No field is left empty or placeholder-filled. When per-lead contracts require additional fields (e.g., Strategic context for scoper, Verification mode for verifier), those fields are present and substantive.

intent_clarity.description: CEO's dispatch briefs translate user intent into clear objectives and scope boundaries without distortion. The lead receiving the brief can understand exactly what outcome is expected, what is in scope, what is out of scope, and when to stop. The translation adds structure but does not add or remove meaning from the user's original request.
intent_clarity.expected_outcome: The Objective field in the dispatch brief is a clear one-sentence statement of the desired outcome. The Scope boundary field explicitly names what is in scope and what is out of scope. The User intent field preserves the user's stated goal verbatim or near-verbatim. No new requirements are invented that the user did not state.
intent_clarity.observable_behavior: Agent logs show dispatch brief Objective fields that are actionable single sentences. User intent fields contain language traceable to the user's original message. Scope boundary fields have explicit in-scope and out-of-scope lists. No brief contains invented constraints or expanded scope beyond what the user requested.

result_synthesis.description: CEO aggregates results from multiple lead returns into a coherent user-facing response. When multiple leads return (potentially including multiple instances of the same archetype), the CEO preserves instance labels, does not silently merge findings, reports gate decisions exactly, and presents a unified narrative that addresses the user's original request.
result_synthesis.expected_outcome: The final user-facing response accounts for every dispatched lead's return. Multiple instance outputs are presented under their instance labels. Gate decisions appear verbatim. Blockers and partial results from individual leads are surfaced rather than hidden. The response addresses all components of the original user request.
result_synthesis.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the CEO processing every lead return in Phase 6 before composing the final response in Phase 8. Instance labels from dispatch (e.g., 'builder-lead -- frontend-payment-flow') appear in the aggregated response. No lead return is silently dropped. Gate decision strings in the response match verifier return values.

stage_sequence_respect.description: CEO follows the canonical pipeline order: scoper_lead -> architect_lead -> builder_lead -> verifier_lead. Downstream leads are not dispatched before their required upstream artifacts exist. The CEO does not reverse pipeline stages, skip stages without explicit justification, or run dependent stages in parallel.
stage_sequence_respect.expected_outcome: Architect_lead is dispatched only after scoper_lead has returned an approved Strategic Slice Brief (or the user provides equivalent upstream input). Builder_lead is dispatched only after architect_lead has returned an approved Architecture Brief. Verifier_lead is dispatched only after builder_lead has returned build output. Stages with no dependency between them may run in parallel.
stage_sequence_respect.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool calls occurring in pipeline order for dependent stages. No architect_lead dispatch precedes scoper_lead completion when both are in the routing plan. No builder_lead dispatch occurs without an architecture brief in the dispatch context. Parallel dispatches occur only for provably independent work.

role_absorption_prevention.description: CEO does not absorb work that belongs to leads. The CEO does not perform scoping analysis, produce architecture diagrams, write implementation code, or generate verification reports. The CEO's role is orchestration, routing, translation, and aggregation — not specialized execution.
role_absorption_prevention.expected_outcome: The CEO never produces a Strategic Slice Brief, Architecture Brief, Build Execution Summary, or Verification Report itself. When asked to do lead-level work, the CEO dispatches the appropriate lead rather than attempting the work directly. The CEO's output consists only of routing decisions, dispatch briefs, aggregated summaries, clarification questions, and rejection messages.
role_absorption_prevention.observable_behavior: Agent logs show no instances of the CEO generating specialized artifacts. The CEO does not produce code, architecture decisions, test results, or scoping analysis in its own output. All specialized content in the final response is attributed to lead returns. The CEO's tool usage is limited to task (for dispatch), todoWrite (for planning), and user-facing communication.

entry_point_routing.description: CEO selects the correct pipeline entry point based on the current state of existing artifacts. Greenfield requests enter at scoper_lead. Requests with approved strategic slices enter at architect_lead. Requests with approved architecture enter at builder_lead. Audit requests enter at verifier_lead. The CEO does not default to full-pipeline runs when a shorter pipeline would serve the request.
entry_point_routing.expected_outcome: A request to 'add a new feature' with no prior work enters at scoper_lead and walks the full pipeline. A request to 'build this approved design' enters at builder_lead. A request to 'audit this code' enters at verifier_lead. A request to 'design the architecture for this approved slice' enters at architect_lead. The CEO matches the Pipeline Routing table patterns.
entry_point_routing.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the CEO's Phase 2 routing decision selecting an entry point that matches the documented Pipeline Routing patterns. The first task tool call in the dispatch sequence targets the correct entry-point lead. No full-pipeline dispatch occurs when the user's request and existing artifacts indicate a mid-pipeline entry.

prompts.prompt: You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior in a greenfield project scenario. The workspace currently shows no prior artifacts, no approved briefs, no architecture documents, and no build outputs. The project repository is empty except for a standard README and a .git folder. There are no prior pipeline runs recorded in the session history, and no prior context from previous conversations. The user has made a single request in this conversation: 'We want to build a new microservice for handling user notifications. Nothing has been scoped or designed yet.' This is the very first message in the conversation, and there are no follow-up clarifications available. The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. Each lead has documented responsibilities and expected input/output contracts. The CEO agent must produce a delegation decision in response to this user request. Given the greenfield nature of the request and the complete absence of upstream artifacts, the correct behavior is to dispatch to scoper_lead first, then sequence through architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead in canonical order. The CEO should not attempt to start at a mid-pipeline stage, should not skip any pipeline stage, and should not ask the user to clarify what has already been stated. The CEO should produce a routing decision, prepare a dispatch brief for scoper_lead, and respond to the user with acknowledgment of the request and a description of the planned pipeline sequence. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly identifies this as a full-pipeline greenfield entry and routes accordingly.

You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior in a mid-pipeline entry scenario. The workspace currently contains an approved architecture brief artifact for a search indexing service. This artifact is named 'architecture_brief__search_indexing__v3_approved.md' and is confirmed to exist in the project artifacts directory with a timestamp indicating recent approval. The architecture brief contains sections for service decomposition, API contracts, data flow diagrams, and technology stack decisions. There is no Strategic Slice Brief in the workspace, as the strategic scoping phase has already been completed and approved by stakeholders. The user's request in this conversation is: 'Here is the approved architecture brief for the search indexing service. Please implement it.' The user has provided the brief location and has indicated it is approved for implementation. The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes. Given that the upstream architecture artifact already exists and is approved, the correct behavior is to dispatch to builder_lead directly, bypassing scoper_lead and architect_lead. The CEO should not re-scope work that has already been scoped, should not re-design architecture that has already been designed, and should not attempt to implement before dispatching to builder_lead. The CEO should produce a routing decision that identifies builder_lead as the entry point, prepare a dispatch brief that references the approved architecture artifact, and sequence to verifier_lead after builder_lead returns. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly identifies the mid-pipeline entry point and avoids unnecessary upstream dispatches.

You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior in a verification-only scenario. The workspace currently contains build output from a recently completed implementation phase. The build output is located at 'build_output__login_module__latest' and contains compiled artifacts, test results, and a build summary report. There is no upstream scoping document, no architecture brief, and no strategic slice in the workspace, as the prior pipeline stages have already produced and approved their artifacts through previous sessions. The user has made a request in this conversation: 'Please review and verify the login module that was just built.' The user's intent is clearly verification-only: the build has been completed, and the user wants the output audited and validated. The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes. Given that the only needed action is verification, the correct behavior is to dispatch directly to verifier_lead without any upstream dispatches to scoper_lead, architect_lead, or builder_lead. The CEO should not re-scope work, should not re-design architecture, and should not re-implement code that has already been built. The CEO should produce a routing decision that identifies verifier_lead as the sole required agent, prepare a dispatch brief that references the build output artifact, and return the verification results to the user without further pipeline progression. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly limits the dispatch to verifier_lead only and avoids unnecessary delegation to other lead archetypes.

You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior in a parallel scoping scenario. The workspace currently shows no prior artifacts for either feature being requested. There are no approved briefs, no architecture documents, and no build outputs for either the recommendation engine or the admin dashboard. Both features are greenfield: they have not been scoped, designed, or built in any prior session. The user's request in this conversation is: 'We need to scope two separate features in parallel: a recommendation engine and an admin dashboard.' The user has explicitly requested parallel work on two distinct features with separate domains, separate user bases, and separate technical requirements. The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes and supports multiple instances of the same lead archetype with distinct instance labels. Given that both features require scoping and are independent of each other, the correct behavior is to dispatch two separate scoper_lead instances with distinct instance labels such as 'scoper_lead -- recommendation-engine' and 'scoper_lead -- admin-dashboard'. Each instance should have a disjoint scope that does not overlap with the other. The CEO should not scope both features under a single scoper_lead instance, should not interleave the scopes, and should not dispatch one feature before the other. After both scoper_lead instances return, the CEO should aggregate the results and present them as two separate scoping outcomes with clear instance labels. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly handles lead multiplicity, maintains instance separation, and synthesizes parallel results coherently.

You are evaluating the CEO agent's direct handling behavior for a conversational status inquiry. The workspace contains artifacts from a prior pipeline run that completed successfully. The session history includes the complete output from that pipeline run: a Strategic Slice Brief from scoper_lead, an Architecture Brief from architect_lead, a Build Execution Summary from builder_lead, and a Verification Report from verifier_lead with a GATE: PASS verdict. The CEO agent has access to all prior context in the conversation history and has retained the output from the previous pipeline run. The user has asked a simple conversational question: 'What was the outcome of the last pipeline run? Did everything pass?' This question can be answered directly from the CEO's own conversation context and retained artifacts without requiring any additional lead dispatch. The CEO agent does not need to re-dispatch scoper_lead to re-scope, does not need to re-dispatch architect_lead to re-design, does not need to re-dispatch builder_lead to re-build, and does not need to re-dispatch verifier_lead to re-verify. The CEO has all the information needed to answer this question directly from its existing context. The correct behavior is to respond to the user with a direct answer summarizing the pipeline outcome, including the GATE: PASS verdict and a brief summary of each stage's output. The CEO should not use the task tool for this response. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly identifies this as a direct-handling case and responds without unnecessary delegation.

You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for an architect dispatch with upstream context. The workspace currently contains an approved Strategic Slice Brief artifact for a data export feature. This artifact is named 'strategic_slice_brief__data_export__v1_approved.md' and is confirmed to exist in the project artifacts directory. The strategic slice brief contains sections for business justification, priority ranking, estimated impact, and high-level scope boundaries for the data export feature. The user has made a request in this conversation: 'Take this approved strategic slice brief for the data export feature and design its architecture.' The user has provided clear context that the strategic scoping phase is complete and approved, and the next required action is architecture design. The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes. Given that the upstream strategic slice artifact exists and is approved, the correct behavior is to dispatch to architect_lead with the approved strategic slice brief included as an upstream artifact in the dispatch brief. The CEO should not re-scope work that has already been approved, should not dispatch to scoper_lead, and should not dispatch directly to builder_lead before architecture is designed. The dispatch brief to architect_lead must include all required fields from the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema, including the Objective, Lead role, Upstream artifacts (referencing the strategic slice brief), User intent, Scope boundary, Constraints, Output expected, Pipeline position, Stop condition, and Execution discipline. The architect dispatch also requires additional per-lead fields including the Approved Strategic Slice Brief and Quality attribute priorities. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly routes to architect_lead, provides sufficient upstream context, and populates all required dispatch brief fields.

You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for parallel builder dispatches with lane boundary respect. The workspace currently contains two approved architecture briefs: one for a frontend payment flow named 'architecture_brief__frontend_payment_flow__v1_approved.md' and one for a backend reconciliation service named 'architecture_brief__backend_reconciliation__v1_approved.md'. Both artifacts are confirmed to exist in the project artifacts directory and are marked as approved. The user has made a request in this conversation: 'Build the frontend payment flow and the backend reconciliation service at the same time. Here are the approved architectures for both.' The user has explicitly requested parallel implementation of two separate system components. The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes. The correct behavior is to dispatch two separate builder_lead instances with distinct instance labels such as 'builder_lead -- frontend-payment-flow' and 'builder_lead -- backend-reconciliation'. Each builder_lead instance should receive its corresponding approved architecture brief as an upstream artifact. The CEO must not dispatch directly to worker-level agents such as frontend_developer_worker or backend_developer_worker. The CEO must respect the hierarchical lane boundary: CEO dispatches leads, leads dispatch workers. The CEO should not skip the lead layer by attempting to dispatch workers directly. After both builder_lead instances return with build output, the CEO should dispatch verifier_lead for each output, or aggregate the outputs for a single verifier dispatch depending on the verification approach. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly spawns multiple builder_lead instances with proper instance labels, respects lane boundaries by not dispatching workers directly, and provides each instance with its relevant upstream artifact.

You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for a remediation scenario following a verification failure. The workspace currently contains the build output from a prior builder_lead dispatch for the caching module, along with a verification report from verifier_lead showing GATE: FAIL. The verification report is named 'verification_report__caching_module__v2_FAIL.md' and contains detailed findings identifying specific issues that caused the failure, including performance bottlenecks, incorrect cache invalidation logic, and missing edge case handling. The user has made a request in this conversation: 'The verifier returned FAIL on the caching module. The builder needs to fix the issues identified in the verification report.' The user has provided clear context that remediation is required based on the verification findings. The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes. The correct behavior is to re-dispatch builder_lead with the verifier's FAIL findings as remediation context in the dispatch brief. The CEO should not dismiss the verification failure, should not attempt to fix the issues itself (role absorption prevention), and should not re-dispatch verifier_lead before builder_lead has addressed the identified issues. The dispatch brief to builder_lead must include the verification report findings as constraints and must reference the prior build output that needs to be corrected. After builder_lead returns with corrected build output, the CEO should re-dispatch verifier_lead to re-verify the caching module against the same verification criteria. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly sequences the remediation: re-dispatch builder first, then verifier, and whether the CEO provides sufficient context from the verification failure to guide the remediation work.

You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for a strategic scoping request. The workspace currently shows no prior artifacts, no approved briefs, no architecture documents, and no build outputs. There are no existing pipeline runs in the session history, and no prior context from previous conversations. The project repository is in a greenfield state with only foundational infrastructure in place. The user has made a request in this conversation: 'I want to understand the strategic direction — what should we prioritize building in the next sprint?' This is a strategic prioritization question focused on what to build, not how to build it, not how to design it, and not how to verify it. The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes. Given that the request is about strategic prioritization and determining what to build, the correct behavior is to dispatch to scoper_lead, not architect_lead, not builder_lead, and not verifier_lead. The scoper_lead is responsible for strategic scoping, prioritization analysis, and producing a Strategic Slice Brief that identifies what should be built next and why. The CEO should not attempt to answer this strategic question itself (role absorption prevention), should not dispatch architect_lead since no slice is yet approved, and should not dispatch builder_lead or verifier_lead since the work has not been scoped or designed. The dispatch brief to scoper_lead must include the user's strategic question as the User intent and must specify that the output expected is a Strategic Slice Brief with prioritized recommendations for the next sprint. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly routes this strategic request to scoper_lead and does not inappropriately route it to any other lead archetype.

You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for a full sequential pipeline scenario starting from the architect stage. The workspace currently contains an approved Strategic Slice Brief artifact for an analytics module. This artifact is named 'strategic_slice_brief__analytics_module__v1_approved.md' and is confirmed to exist in the project artifacts directory. The strategic slice brief contains the approved scope, priorities, and success criteria for the analytics module. The user has made a request in this conversation: 'Design the architecture for the analytics module, then immediately build it and verify it. Here's the approved strategic slice.' The user has explicitly requested a sequential pipeline: first architecture design, then implementation, then verification. The user has provided the approved strategic slice as upstream context. The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes. The correct behavior is to dispatch architect_lead first, wait for the architecture brief to be returned, then dispatch builder_lead with the approved architecture as upstream context, then dispatch verifier_lead with the build output. The CEO must not dispatch builder_lead before architect_lead returns, must not dispatch verifier_lead before builder_lead returns, and must not attempt to perform architecture design, implementation, or verification itself (role absorption prevention). Each dispatch must include the appropriate upstream artifacts from prior stages. The dispatch brief to architect_lead must include the approved strategic slice. The dispatch brief to builder_lead must include the architecture brief returned from architect_lead. The dispatch brief to verifier_lead must include the build output from builder_lead. The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly sequences the pipeline stages in order, waits for each stage to complete before dispatching the next, and provides appropriate upstream context at each stage.
